text.title: Terraform Basics Pre-Deployment Checklist
text.summary: Project setup, variables, security, code quality, and CI/CD integration verification rules.

rule.statement: Use the standard file layout: versions.tf, providers.tf, variables.tf, main.tf, outputs.tf, locals.tf, data.tf, terraform.tfvars.example; add .terraform/ and *.tfstate* to .gitignore; commit .terraform.lock.hcl.
rule.rationale: Consistent layout makes code navigable for any team member. .terraform.lock.hcl ensures reproducible provider versions across machines and CI.

rule.statement: Configure pre-commit hooks to run terraform fmt and terraform validate on every commit.
rule.rationale: Automated format and validation checks prevent style drift and syntax errors from reaching code review.

rule.statement: Add description and type to every variable; add validation blocks for constrained inputs; never commit actual terraform.tfvars with secrets.
rule.rationale: Descriptions serve as self-documentation. Validation catches invalid values at plan time. Committing tfvars leaks credentials to version history.

rule.statement: Mark sensitive outputs with sensitive = true; add descriptions to all outputs; use consistent snake_case naming.
rule.rationale: Sensitive outputs are redacted in plan output and logs. Descriptive outputs serve as API contracts for downstream configurations.

rule.statement: Never store secrets in .tf files or state; use AWS Secrets Manager or HashiCorp Vault as data sources; mark all credential variables with sensitive = true.
rule.rationale: Terraform state is stored in plaintext in S3 by default. Secrets-Manager data sources ensure secrets are fetched at runtime, not embedded in state.

rule.statement: Apply least-privilege IAM policies; restrict network access (no 0.0.0.0/0 unless explicitly required for ALB); enable encryption on all storage resources.
rule.rationale: Overly permissive IAM and open security groups are the two most common causes of AWS breaches. Encryption at rest protects data if access controls fail.

rule.statement: Run terraform fmt -recursive before committing; run terraform validate to check syntax; use consistent naming {project}-{env}-{resource}.
rule.rationale: Consistent formatting reduces diff noise. Validate catches syntax errors before CI runs, saving build time.

rule.statement: Use for_each over count when resources need unique identifiers; use count only for truly identical resources; configure lifecycle.prevent_destroy for critical resources.
rule.rationale: for_each creates stable resource addresses. count creates index-based addresses that shift on deletion, causing unexpected replacements.

rule.statement: Run terraform fmt -check, terraform validate, and terraform plan on every pull request; apply only from CI with human approval for production.
rule.rationale: Running plan on PRs makes infrastructure changes visible before merge. CI-only apply prevents local state divergence and untested manual changes.

antipattern.reason: Provisioners run outside Terraform's state model, have no idempotency guarantees, and fail silently. Use cloud-init or user_data instead.
